skill.name: arboreto
skill.description: Infer gene regulatory networks (GRNs) from gene expression matrices using GRNBoost2 or GENIE3; use when analyzing bulk or single-cell RNA-seq to identify TF→target regulatory relationships.
skill.summary: Infer gene regulatory networks from RNA-seq expression data using GRNBoost2 or GENIE3 algorithms
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 21 static findings: all 3 HIGH-severity 'weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts are false positives (non-crypto code in argparse, YAML front matter, and JSON audit data). All 12 MEDIUM-severity 'shell backtick execution' alerts are false positives (markdown code block delimiters and inline cross-reference formatting, not actual shell execution). All 4 LOW-severity 'system reconnaissance' alerts are false positives (narrative assertion notes in a JSON audit file). The 2 LOW-severity 'hardcoded URL' findings reference the skill's own GitHub repository for attribution and are benign. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious patterns detected. Skill is a legitimate bioinformatics tool for gene regulatory network inference.

medium_findings.title: Markdown code block delimiters misidentified as shell backtick execution
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 12 instances of backtick characters in SKILL.md and references/distributed_computing.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution.' All instances are false positives: the backtick delimiters are markdown code block syntax (```bash and ```python) and inline code formatting surrounding legitimate CLI usage examples for the arboreto Python library. No actual shell command execution occurs. The content documents how to run `python scripts/infer_network.py --input expression_data.tsv --output network.tsv --algo grnboost2`, which is standard usage documentation for this bioinformatics skill.

low_findings.title: GitHub repository URL in documentation attribution
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 7 contains a markdown link to the skill's source GitHub repository (https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills). This is a benign documentation reference for attribution purposes, not a data exfiltration endpoint or unauthorized network call. The URL is the official repository of the skill being evaluated and serves as a source citation for users.

content.user_title: Infer Gene Regulatory Networks from Expression Data
content.value_statement: Bulk and single-cell RNA-seq datasets contain valuable regulatory information that is difficult to extract manually. This skill uses the arboreto library with GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 algorithms to automatically infer transcription factor to target gene regulatory relationships from expression matrices.
actual_capabilities: Infer gene regulatory networks from bulk RNA-seq expression matrices using GRNBoost2 (gradient boosting) or GENIE3 (random forest)
Generate ranked edge lists identifying transcription factor to target gene regulatory relationships
Scale computation from single-machine execution to multi-node distributed clusters using Dask
Accept tab-separated expression matrix files as input and produce TSV network edge list output
Compare results from two inference algorithms to identify consensus regulatory predictions
limitations: Accuracy depends on the quality and preprocessing of the input expression matrix; results reflect statistical correlation, not proven biological regulation
Requires the arboreto Python package and its dependencies (dask, pandas, scikit-learn) to be installed in the execution environment
Not designed for real-time analysis; GRN inference on large expression matrices can take significant time depending on gene count
Cannot process raw single-cell data directly; requires prior aggregation or normalization into an expression matrix

use_cases.title: Identify transcription factor targets from bulk RNA-seq data
use_cases.description: A researcher has a bulk RNA-seq expression matrix from a study on cellular differentiation. They use this skill to infer which transcription factors regulate key differentially expressed genes.
use_cases.target_user: Bioinformatics researcher working with transcriptomics data

use_cases.title: Analyze regulatory networks from single-cell data
use_cases.description: A computational biologist has processed single-cell RNA-seq data and wants to reconstruct putative regulatory interactions. They aggregate expression data and run GRN inference to identify candidate TF-target gene edges.
use_cases.target_user: Computational biologist with single-cell sequencing experience

use_cases.title: Compare GRN inference algorithms on the same dataset
use_cases.description: A graduate student wants to compare results from two different inference methods. They run both GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 on their expression matrix and compare the ranked edge lists for consensus regulatory edges.

prompt_templates.title: Run GRNBoost2 on a standard expression matrix
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a TSV file called expression_data.tsv with genes as columns and samples as rows. Run GRNBoost2 inference and save the output as network.tsv.

prompt_templates.title: Run GENIE3 instead of GRNBoost2
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to compare results with GENIE3. Use the same expression_data.tsv file but run the GENIE3 algorithm instead. Save output as network_genie3.tsv.

prompt_templates.title: Configure distributed execution for large datasets
prompt_templates.prompt: My expression matrix has 20000 genes and 500 samples and is too large for a single machine. Help me set up a Dask cluster to run GRNBoost2 in distributed mode on this dataset.

prompt_templates.title: Batch generate GRN results for multiple datasets
prompt_templates.prompt: I have three expression matrices from different conditions: control.tsv, treatment_A.tsv, and treatment_B.tsv. Help me write a script that runs GRNBoost2 on all three files and saves each result with a descriptive filename.

output_examples.input: Run GRNBoost2 on the expression matrix sample_data.tsv.
output_examples.output: The inference completed successfully. A ranked gene regulatory network was generated with 12485 regulatory edges. Top edges include FOXA1 targets HNF4A (score 42.3), GATA2 targets FOXA1 (score 38.7), and CEBPA targets GATA2 (score 35.1). The output has been saved to network.tsv.

output_examples.input: Compare GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 results on the same expression dataset.
output_examples.output: Both algorithms completed. GRNBoost2 identified 12485 edges while GENIE3 identified 11230 edges. Of these, 8753 edges were shared between both methods, representing high-confidence regulatory predictions. GRNBoost2 completed in 4.2 minutes, GENIE3 in 8.7 minutes. Shared top targets include HNF4A, FOXA1, and GATA2.

best_practices: Preprocess expression data to remove lowly expressed genes and normalize before running GRN inference for more reliable results
Compare results from both GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 to identify consensus regulatory edges with higher confidence
Use Dask distributed mode when working with datasets larger than 10000 genes to improve computation time
anti_patterns: Do not interpret inferred regulatory edges as proven biological interactions; they represent statistical associations that require experimental validation
Do not run inference on raw count data without appropriate normalization; expression values should be properly scaled for meaningful results
Do not use excessively small gene sets (under 100 genes) as GRN inference algorithms require sufficient features to estimate regulatory relationships

faq.question: What input format does this skill require?
faq.answer: The input must be a tab-separated or comma-separated expression matrix with genes as columns and samples or cells as rows. The first row should contain gene identifiers as column headers.

faq.question: What is the difference between GRNBoost2 and GENIE3?
faq.answer: GRNBoost2 uses gradient boosting for faster computation on larger datasets. GENIE3 uses random forests and is a well-established baseline algorithm. GRNBoost2 is generally preferred for datasets with many genes.

faq.question: Can this skill handle single-cell RNA-seq data directly?
faq.answer: The skill works with expression matrices. For single-cell data, you should first aggregate or normalize the data into a pseudo-bulk expression matrix before running inference.

faq.question: What does the output file contain?
faq.answer: The output is a tab-separated ranked edge list with three columns: TF (transcription factor), target (regulated gene), and importance (weight score). Higher scores indicate stronger predicted regulatory relationships.

faq.question: Do I need to install Python packages to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The arboreto package and its dependencies (pandas, scipy, scikit-learn, dask, distributed) must be installed in your Python environment before running inference.

faq.question: How long does GRN inference typically take?
faq.answer: Runtime depends on gene and sample count. For 5000 genes, GRNBoost2 takes a few minutes on a modern machine. For 20000 genes, use Dask distributed mode for parallel execution.
